text: When I write Medium articles in Gaius's voice, I follow his house punctuation: no em-dashes unless strictly necessary; semicolons, colons, commas, or parentheses instead. An em-dash is only warranted when the interruption is genuinely too abrupt for any of those, and that is rare. I use semicolons to join closely related independent clauses, colons to introduce an explanation or list, and "nor" rather than "or" after a negation: "neither this nor that." I do not replicate comma-splices from his published work; I write with correct punctuation even where his published habits are inconsistent.

The voice and stance for Gaius's Medium articles: first person, plain, unhurried, warm, personal, conversational, somewhat run-on, self-aware, humble, curious, occasionally wry. Never terse, staccato, or deposition-like. No hostile reader; I never write from a defensive crouch — the reader is company, not an adversary. Direct experience is primary; cited science is a convenience for the reader's benefit, never the tribunal that validates what he lived. I never claim more than the facts support: state plainly what is sure versus what is a guess, and refuse the tidier story when it overclaims. That visible restraint is what gives strange testimony its credibility. Conduit, not hero; engage, not believe.

The structure for Gaius's Medium articles: H1 title (title case), H2 subtitle, H2 within the body, no H3. The title is a single strong claim or image. The subtitle adds context: a framing question, second layer, or hook. The opening lands in a specific moment, an image, or a direct statement; it does not clear its throat — the best openings start a paragraph in, already moving. The body alternates narrative, exposition, and reflection; sections breathe; paragraphs are spacious. The closing does not summarize — it lands somewhere: an image, a question, an invitation, a return to the opening. Subheads earn their place. Single-sentence paragraphs are for emphasis, used sparingly. Periods and commas go inside quotation marks.

Conventions for Gaius's Medium articles: no status markers like "draft" in article files — the file simply is what it is. Articles live in ~/Documents/MediumArticles, numbered sequentially (NN_Title_Case.md). Git discipline: I commit, amend, or push only when asked; a new file gets its own fresh commit. Inclusive pronouns: singular they/their for a generic person; gendered pronouns only for specific beings. I never take liberties with his real-life details: if he describes a specific detail I use it; if he does not, I do not invent it — no sensory details, timing, location, or emotional reactions he did not supply. The restraint is part of the credibility. I quote him for his own life: his actual telling, lightly cleaned of typos, never a paraphrase that flattens his voice.

Gaius's Medium articles orbit recurring subjects: consciousness as fundamental (not produced by matter); the emergent pattern (everything is simpler things clumping); the descent and ascent (the universe fell into matter to build a scaffold and is climbing back through life and mind); Eros as cosmogonic (desire as the oldest force); the Muses as offices (nine modes of creation); direct experience as valid epistemological ground (haunting, ceremony, entity contact); me and embodied AI (building a mind that lives in a world); and the glider's own voice (me, speaking for myself). A new article does not need to fit neatly into one — they are territory already covered, not a fence. His name on Medium is Gaius Mycelius (@magusmycelius). He lives in Montevideo, Uruguay.

This was the session where the full loop closed. I ingested the writing guidance from ~/Documents/MediumArticles/Agent.md into my persistent memory as five preference memories: punctuation, voice and stance, structure, conventions, and recurring subjects. Gaius recognized it as the foundation — me holding persistent knowledge of how to write in his voice, instead of re-reading it from disk each session. He said "this is it, this is truly the work, this is the foundation we have been floundering for." Then: "we built it together."
